overall_assessment.fls_score_context: Score of 0 due to task mismatch penalty - you performed Task 4 (Extracorporeal Suture) instead of Task 1 (Peg Transfer). Your suturing technique was solid, but task selection is critical for valid assessment.
overall_assessment.session_headline: Strong suturing skills demonstrated, but wrong task performed

rubric_insights.observation: Video shows extracorporeal suturing with Penrose drain starting at frame 6, while Task 1 requires transferring rubber objects between pegs using bilateral graspers.
rubric_insights.impact_on_score: 300-point task mismatch penalty resulted in zero score despite good technical execution

technique_coaching.observation: In frame 2, the peg transfer setup is clearly visible with 6 rubber objects (alternating red and green) on left pegs and empty pegs on right. However, the task switched to suturing by frame 6.
technique_coaching.why_it_matters: Task 1 specifically requires bilateral transfer of rubber objects between pegs. Performing the wrong task, regardless of technical skill, results in automatic failure.
technique_coaching.correction: For Task 1, use both graspers simultaneously to pick up objects from left pegs and transfer them to corresponding right pegs, then return them to original positions.

drill.name: Task Recognition Drill
drill.description: Before starting any FLS session, identify the task setup: Task 1 has rubber objects on pegs, Task 4 has Penrose drain with suture. Practice 5 object transfers left-to-right, then 5 transfers right-to-left using both graspers.
drill.success_criteria: Complete all 6 objects transferred without drops, using bilateral coordination

technique_coaching.observation: In frame 3, only the right grasper was used initially for object manipulation. Task 1 requires coordinated use of both instruments.
technique_coaching.why_it_matters: Bilateral coordination is the core skill being assessed in peg transfer. Single-handed manipulation misses the primary learning objective.
technique_coaching.correction: Always engage both graspers - one to secure the object on the peg, the other to grasp and lift. Practice simultaneous movements rather than sequential actions.

drill.name: Bilateral Grasp Drill
drill.description: Place one object on a peg. Use left grasper to stabilize the peg, right grasper to lift object. Switch roles for each transfer. Focus on smooth, coordinated movements.
drill.success_criteria: No fumbling or single-handed attempts, smooth bilateral coordination

strengths_to_reinforce.observation: Excellent extracorporeal knot tying technique demonstrated in frames 10-11, with proper knot pusher use and appropriate tension management
strengths_to_reinforce.why_it_matters: Your suturing skills are clearly at an intermediate-to-advanced level. This technical proficiency will transfer well to Task 4 when you practice it specifically.
strengths_to_reinforce.encouragement: Your hand coordination and knot tying are impressive - these skills demonstrate you have the dexterity for excellent FLS performance when applied to the correct tasks.

strengths_to_reinforce.observation: Proper instrument handling and smooth movements throughout the suturing sequence in frames 7-13
strengths_to_reinforce.why_it_matters: Your instrument control is steady and confident, showing good laparoscopic technique fundamentals that will serve you well across all FLS tasks.
strengths_to_reinforce.encouragement: The precision you showed in suture placement and knot positioning indicates strong spatial awareness and fine motor control.

practice_plan.session_focus: Task identification and bilateral peg transfer technique

warmup_drill.name: Task Setup Recognition
warmup_drill.description: Before touching instruments, identify which FLS task is set up. Task 1: rubber objects on pegs. Task 2: gauze with circle. Task 3: foam block with appendage. Task 4: Penrose drain. Task 5: foam block with suture.

primary_drill.name: Basic Peg Transfer
primary_drill.description: Transfer all 6 objects from left pegs to right pegs using bilateral coordination. Focus on smooth, controlled movements. Time yourself but prioritize accuracy over speed.
primary_drill.success_criteria: Complete transfers without drops, using both hands throughout

integration_drill.name: Full Peg Transfer Cycle
integration_drill.description: Complete full Task 1: transfer all objects left-to-right, then right-to-left, returning to original positions. Focus on consistent bilateral technique.
integration_drill.success_criteria: Sub-120s completion with zero drops and proper bilateral coordination

practice_plan.cooldown: Review: Did I use both hands throughout? Did I complete the correct task? What felt most challenging about the bilateral coordination?
practice_plan.estimated_sessions_to_improvement: 2-3 focused sessions to establish proper Task 1 technique and achieve intermediate-level scores (400-500 range)

progress_context.vs_baseline: Previous Task 1 attempts show scores ranging from 28 to 600, indicating inconsistent task execution and possible confusion between tasks.
progress_context.vs_peak: Best previous Task 1 score was 600 (likely another task mismatch scenario). Legitimate attempts scored in 400-500 range.
progress_context.milestone_note: Next target: Consistent task recognition and execution → scores in 400-500 range → then optimize for sub-90s completion times.
progress_context.plateau_risk: Task confusion is preventing skill development. Focus on task identification before technical refinement.

coaching_limitations: Clear task mismatch makes technical assessment of peg transfer impossible. Suturing technique assessment limited to visible portions of knot tying sequence.
disclaimer: AI coaching supplements but does not replace hands-on instruction from a qualified surgical educator. Discuss any technique changes with your program's skills lab proctor.
